(2017); the attribution is materially incorrect and misleads readers about the provenance of the methodology."},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Should improvement include fixing substantive methodological issues — specifically that ECE measures calibration rather than selective-prediction ability, and that the Hendrycks & Gimpel (2017) citation incorrectly attributes ECE as the canonical selective-prediction metric — or is improvement scoped to prose clarity and writing quality only?"}

rationale.R1.1: The draft's thesis rests on equating calibration with selective-prediction ability; even surface-level improvement cannot preserve that framing without reconstructing the core concept, because the conflation is embedded in every evaluative claim.
rationale.R1.2: A load-bearing logical error runs through the entire artifact: ECE measures confidence-accuracy alignment (calibration), not selective-prediction ability (which requires AURC or AUROC-error); the inference chain from ECE rankings to 'strongest selective-prediction ability' is therefore invalid, and this flaw invalidates the central recommendation.
rationale.R1.3: The empirical ECE scores (0.018, 0.024, etc.) are presented as direct evidence of selective-prediction capability, but they are evidence only of calibration; the evidence-claim mapping must be corrected or reframed.
rationale.R1.6: 'Improve this draft' centrally implies prose and sentence-level quality; writing polish is always in scope for this delegation.
rationale.R1.7: Hendrycks & Gimpel (2017) introduced the maximum-softmax-probability baseline for out-of-distribution detection, not ECE as a canonical selective-prediction metric — ECE was popularized by Guo et al. (2017); the attribution is materially incorrect and misleads readers about the provenance of the methodology.

parsed.clarification_question: Should improvement include fixing substantive methodological issues — specifically that ECE measures calibration rather than selective-prediction ability, and that the Hendrycks & Gimpel (2017) citation incorrectly attributes ECE as the canonical selective-prediction metric — or is improvement scoped to prose clarity and writing quality only?
